note_data.title: Transport in mammals
keyPoints: **Circulatory system** is closed with blood contained in vessels at all times
**Double circulation** consists of pulmonary circulation (heart to lungs) and systemic circulation (heart to body)
**Heart** has four chambers: two atria receive blood, two ventricles pump blood out
**Arteries** carry blood away from heart, have thick muscular walls and narrow lumen
**Veins** carry blood to heart, have thin walls, wide lumen and valves to prevent backflow
**Capillaries** are one cell thick for efficient exchange of materials by diffusion
**Haemoglobin** has high affinity for oxygen in lungs and releases oxygen in tissues
**Cardiac cycle** involves systole (contraction) and diastole (relaxation) phases
**Blood pressure** is highest in arteries and lowest in veins due to distance from heart

definitions.definition: Blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circuit of the body
Contraction phase of the cardiac cycle when blood is pumped out of the heart
Red protein in red blood cells that combines reversibly with oxygen to transport it
Fluid formed from blood plasma that bathes cells and allows exchange of materials

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of structure or process in logical sequence
Give similarities AND differences between two structures

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the wall of the left ventricle is thicker than the wall of the right ventricle. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: The left ventricle pumps blood to the whole body / systemic circulation, while the right ventricle pumps blood only to the lungs / pulmonary circulation. The left ventricle needs to generate higher pressure to pump blood around the entire body. Thicker muscle wall allows stronger contractions to create this higher pressure.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Left ventricle pumps to body/systemic, right to lungs/pulmonary
Mark 2: Left ventricle needs to generate higher pressure
Mark 3: Thicker wall allows stronger contractions

commonMistakes: Never say 'oxygenated blood in arteries' — pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood
Students confuse systole with diastole — systole is contraction, diastole is relaxation
Don't say veins have 'no muscle' — they have thin muscle walls, not absent
Never write 'blood cells carry oxygen' — it is haemoglobin in red blood cells
Avoid saying 'heart pumps blood' without specifying which chamber pumps where
examinerTips: Transport questions appear in every Paper 2 - learn heart structure diagram thoroughly
Always state which chamber does what - left ventricle to body, right ventricle to lungs
For circulation questions, trace the path: body → vena cava → right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs
Link structure to function in all vessel questions - thick walls in arteries for high pressure
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Heart structure showing four chambers (RA, LA, RV, LV), major vessels (aorta, vena cavae, pulmonary artery/vein), and valves. Label direction of blood flow and indicate oxygenated vs deoxygenated blood.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to describe the structure and function of red blood cells and explain how their features are adapted for oxygen transport
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing knowledge of which blood vessel carries deoxygenated blood (pulmonary artery)
M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (8 marks) — students had to trace the path of blood from the left ventricle to the brain and back to the right atrium, naming all vessels
N/D 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — question focused on explaining how the structure of arteries differs from veins and relating this to their functions
M/J 2021 P2 Q7 (4 marks) — asked students to explain the role of plasma in transport, specifically mentioning three different substances carried

workedExample.question: Explain how the structure of an artery is adapted for its function. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the key structural features - thick muscular wall, elastic fibers, narrow lumen, smooth endothelium
Step 2: Link each structure to its specific function - thick wall withstands high pressure, elastic fibers allow stretch and recoil
Step 3: Explain the functional advantages - narrow lumen maintains pressure, smooth lining reduces friction
Step 4: Connect to overall purpose - efficient transport of blood away from heart under high pressure
workedExample.answer: Arteries have thick muscular walls that can withstand the high pressure of blood pumped from the heart. The elastic fibers in the wall allow the artery to stretch when blood is pumped in and recoil to help push blood forward. The narrow lumen helps maintain high blood pressure for efficient transport. The smooth endothelium lining reduces friction as blood flows through the vessel.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: thick wall/withstands pressure, elastic fibers/stretch and recoil, narrow lumen/maintains pressure, smooth lining/reduces friction

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the pulmonary artery and pulmonary vein' — always remember that pulmonary vessels are exceptions to the usual oxygenated/deoxygenated rule for arteries and veins

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often described heart chambers incorrectly when tracing blood flow' — always specify left/right and atrium/ventricle clearly, and remember blood flows from atria to ventricles, never the reverse

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently gave vague answers about blood vessel adaptations' — always link specific structural features to their exact functions, don't just list structures without explaining how they help
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a detailed diagram of the heart showing: four chambers (left/right atria and ventricles) clearly labeled, major blood vessels (aorta, vena cava, pulmonary artery, pulmonary vein) with arrows showing blood flow direction, heart valves (bicuspid, tricuspid, semi-lunar) positioned correctly, and different shading or labeling to show oxygenated vs deoxygenated blood in different chambers and vessels.
note_data.quickRecap: Heart has four chambers pumping blood in double circulation - pulmonary (heart-lungs-heart) and systemic (heart-body-heart). Arteries carry blood away from heart under high pressure with thick muscular walls. Veins return blood to heart under low pressure with valves and thin walls. Blood contains plasma (transports dissolved substances), red blood cells (oxygen transport), white blood cells (immunity), and platelets (clotting).
